text: The surgeon begins the robotic-assisted radical prostatectomy by accessing the preperitoneal space from the intraperitoneal cavity. The urachus is divided from the umbilicus, and the preperitoneal plane is developed using sharp and blunt dissection. The dissection continues laterally, posterior to the medial umbilical ligament, utilizing bipolar forceps in the left hand and monopolar scissors in the right hand. Coordination between the assistant and the primary surgeon is visible as they facilitate access into the retropubic space of Retzius.

The surgeon visualizes the pelvic floor and mobilizes the right prevesical space, moving towards the retropubic space of Retzius on the patient's right side. Loose areolar tissue is identified, providing access to the pelvic floor. The shiny tissue on the right side is the endopelvic fascia, with the puboprostatic ligament visible as it secures the prostate to the pubis. The prostate is defatted, revealing the anterior surface and the junction between the prostate and bladder, despite the patient's relative paucity of extraperitoneal fat.

The surgeon begins on the patient's right side, making an incision in the endopelvic fascia. The fibers of the levator ani muscle are reflected off using sharp and blunt dissection, retracting them around to the apex of the prostate. This exposes the puboprostatic ligament, which is divided at its prostatic component while leaving the urethral component intact. The same procedure is repeated on the patient's left side, using diathermy sparingly to access the endopelvic fascia and cauterize any small perforating bleeders from the levator ani.

The surgeon releases the cephalad retraction on the bladder and secures the cut edges of the reflected endopelvic fascia over the prostate in the midline. An O PDS suture on a semicircular needle is passed through the notch between the urethra and the dorsal venous complex (DVC) to the contralateral side, ensuring no bleeding occurs. The suture is secured on the preserved urethral component of the puboprostatic ligament, facilitating hemostatic apical dissection. A slip knot is used, with the suture elevated at ninety degrees to the urethra, and cinched down in a lassoing fashion to control the DVC.

The surgeon secures the suture with additional throws to prevent slippage during the procedure. The suture is divided, the needle is removed, and a second suture is placed through the previously divided uracus to provide cephalad retraction of the bladder, facilitating the dissection of the bladder neck.
